text:
Standardized food reference amounts—such as 100 grams, 100 kilocalories, or the FDA’s Reference Amount Customarily Consumed (RACC)—often misrepresent the actual nutrient density of foods. Relying on these metrics for dietary choices can inadvertently increase the risk of chronic disease by promoting foods that are either excessive in energy or deficient in key nutrients. This study evaluated the nutritional quality and component levels of 8,596 food items across 25 categories using guidelines from the Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC), the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), and a newly proposed assessment procedure. 

Our findings reveal significant vulnerabilities in current regulatory frameworks. Under both FDA and CAC standards, selecting foods to meet targets for "positive" components (such as protein, fiber, vitamins, and minerals) frequently resulted in energy intakes that far exceeded daily requirements. Conversely, the CAC criteria often failed to identify foods that truly satisfy these positive nutrient demands. We also observed "negative" component overages: many foods categorized as suitable under CAC or FDA guidelines actually surpassed recommended limits for cholesterol, sodium, and saturated fat. 

Furthermore, current reference amounts led to paradoxical quality rankings. Due to the limitations of weight- and calorie-based scaling, fast foods (when analyzed by serving size), spices (when analyzed per 100g), and vegetables (when analyzed per 100 kcal) received disproportionately high nutritional quality scores. By contrast, our suggested procedure provides a more reliable framework for children and adults by accounting for energy density and realistic consumption patterns, ensuring that nutrient targets are met without promoting caloric excess or chronic disease risk.
